lesson.menu_title: The Full Presentation -- Opening to Close
lesson.menu_desc: Join the opening, signposting, data, Q&A, and closing into one smooth, polished talk -- the capstone of Block 3 -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 16 -- The Full Presentation, Opening to Close
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Diogo Leal | Aula 16 -- The Full Presentation

listenings.text: Hi Diogo, it is Sophia. You can now open a talk, signpost it, present the data, take questions, and close. Today you join all of those into one smooth, polished presentation. Three things make a talk feel polished. First, pace. Do not rush. Speak slowly and leave a short pause between each part. Second, poise. Stand calm, breathe, and keep your hands steady. The room reads your body before your words. Third, the flow. Use a clear transition between each part, so the talk moves like one story and not five separate pieces. Before the real day, rehearse the whole talk out loud, from the first word to the last. Time it. When you rehearse three or four times, the words feel natural and you engage the room with your eyes, not your slides. Deliver it like a conversation. You are ready. Talk soon.

Good morning, everyone. Thank you for coming. Today I will give you a quick update on Project Atlas. Let me start with where we are, then the numbers, and finally the next steps. So, first, the project is on track. We finished the migration last month. Now, let me show you the numbers. As you can see in the chart, costs dropped by fifteen percent and revenue rose. That is a strong result. Are there any questions before I close? Yes, that is a fair point about the timeline, and the new tool saves us two weeks. To sum up, Atlas is on track, costs are down, and the team is ready for phase two. I appreciate your time, and I will send a brief summary by email. Thank you very much.

extra_audio.text: Diogo gives one full, polished presentation. First, he opens the talk and greets the room: let me start with where we are. Then, he signposts the parts: first the status, then the numbers, finally the next steps. After that, he presents one data point: costs dropped and revenue rose. Then, he takes a question from the room and answers it calmly. Finally, he closes: to sum up, the project is on track, and he thanks the audience with a clear next step.
